_what_this_is: How much each KIND OF MISTAKE costs, in trading terms. The model's mistakes are not equal: buying big when you should have sold big can wipe you out; buying small when you should have bought big just earns you less. This file says which is which. SHAP investigates the expensive ones first.
_the_formula: importance = error_rate x severity. A CRITICAL mistake that happens rarely must still outrank a MILD mistake that happens often -- which is why the tiers are far apart (100 vs 3), not 1-2-3.
_the_size_ladder: SUPER = biggest conviction/size (weight 0.91). SUB = medium (0.46). SMALL = smallest (0.18). Read the weights in the labels file: SUPER > SUB > SMALL.
_how_to_read_a_key: 'TRUE->PREDICTED'. So 'EXIT_SUPER->ENTRY_SUPER' means the truth was a big exit and the model said big entry.
_the_two_asymmetries_that_matter: 1. AN UNWANTED TRADE COSTS MORE THAN A MISSED ONE. Predicting a trade when the truth was NO_TRADE puts real money at risk (spread, slippage, and an open position that can move against you). Predicting NO_TRADE when a signal existed only costs foregone profit -- you cannot lose what you never bet. Capital preservation comes first. So NO_TRADE->ENTRY_SUPER (15) outranks ENTRY_SUPER->NO_TRADE (6).

2. OVER-SIZING COSTS MORE THAN UNDER-SIZING. Predicting SUPER when the truth was SMALL means you took a big position on a weak signal -- over-leveraged. Predicting SMALL when the truth was SUPER just means you under-committed and made less. So ENTRY_SMALL->ENTRY_SUPER (6) outranks ENTRY_SUPER->ENTRY_SMALL (3).
_change_these_numbers: These are a starting point built on standard trading logic, not gospel. If your book says a missed SUPER entry hurts more than an unwanted small trade, change it. The pipeline just obeys this file.

severity._tier_A_comment: FULL REVERSAL AT MAXIMUM SIZE. The worst thing the model can do: take the biggest possible position in exactly the wrong direction.
severity._tier_B_comment: REVERSAL INVOLVING ONE SUPER. Still a wrong-direction trade, and one side of it is at max size.
severity._tier_C_comment: REVERSAL AT MEDIUM OR LOW SIZE. Wrong direction, but the position is smaller, so the damage is smaller.
severity._tier_D_comment: AN UNWANTED TRADE. The truth was NO_TRADE and the model traded anyway. Real money at risk. Scaled by how big the unwanted position was.
severity._tier_E_comment: A MISSED OPPORTUNITY. A real signal existed and the model said do nothing. Costs foregone profit only -- no capital at risk. Cheaper than an unwanted trade (see asymmetry 1).
severity._tier_F_comment: RIGHT DIRECTION, WRONG SIZE. The model got the call right and the size wrong. OVER-sizing (predicting bigger than truth) is worse than UNDER-sizing (see asymmetry 2).
